description.narrative: The proposed Santa Clara Downtown Precise Plan (SCDPP) is a mixed-use development project located in Santa Clara, California. The project includes up to 1,071 residential units and 729,620 square feet of commercial development. The plan aims to complete the street grid in the area by extending Washington, Main, and Franklin Streets.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions in the area are affected by the nearby freeway segments and intersections. The study analyzes the traffic effects of the plan on the key intersections and freeway segments in the vicinity of the site during the weekday AM and PM peak hours of traffic.

issues.location: Lafayette St and El Camino Real
Lafayette St and El Camino Real

recommendations.description: Driveways and alleys of future development should meet the minimum width requirements in the City Code and SCDPP

Future developments should evaluate sight distance at the project driveways/vehicle access points and should be required to prohibit vehicle parking next to the driveways if on-street parking is present near any proposed alleys/driveways
Passenger loading zones should be provided along the fronting street in the multi-use curb lane as planned by the SCDPP
Truck and service vehicles would access the individual developments via the alleys and loading entrances allowed on the streets in the plan area

Future developments in the plan area should be required to minimize curb cuts and implement or contribute to the traffic calming improvements proposed by the SCDPP and the intersection improvements identified in the Pedestrian Master Plan
Future development should provide adequate width of sidewalks along the project frontages based on the SCDPP requirements for streets within the plan area
Future developments should be required to provide secure indoor bicycle parking sufficient to meet resident and employee demand

description.narrative: The Eastside Transit Corridor Phase 2 project aims to improve mobility and enhance accessibility to the regional transit system. The project aligns with applicable plans and ordinances related to transportation policies at both the regional and local jurisdiction level. The project will provide a comprehensive active transportation network and supportive non-infrastructure programs.

known_conditions.narrative: The No Project Alternative would result in no impact related to TRA-3 and TRA-4, as no operations or construction activity would occur. The project was developed to improve mobility and enhance accessibility to the regional transit system.

recommendations.description: Establish a Traffic Management Plan that specifies measures to minimize disruption during construction, such as establishing pedestrian and bicycle detour routes, temporary pedestrian shelters, and wayfinding signage
recommendations.addresses_issue: Disruptions to transit and traffic, pedestrian, and bicycle circulation

metadata.agency: Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority (LSCMTA)

description.narrative: The Norwalk Entertainment District – Civic Center Specific Plan Project is a mixed-use development that includes up to 350 residential units and 110,000 square feet of commercial uses. The project will be completed and operational in Year 2025. The development will occur on the existing City Hall Lawn and the City Hall surface parking lot.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing street system in the Study Area consists of a regional roadway system including freeways, major and secondary highways, and collector and local streets. The speed limits range between 25 and 40 miles per hour on the streets and highways, and between 55 and 65 mph on freeways.

recommendations.description: Implement a comprehensive TDM program, including educational programs, transportation information center, bicycle amenities, promotion and support of carpools and rideshare, incentives for using alternative travel modes, parking incentives, mobility hub support, and community-wide transportation management organization

metadata.report_title: Norwalk Entertainment District – Civic Center Specific Plan Project

description.narrative: The project proposes to replace an existing 30-unit apartment complex with 210 apartment units in downtown San Jose. The project includes a total of 27 levels, with a basement parking level, an at-grade level with parking, freight loading spaces, and residential amenities. Vehicular access to the project parking garage would be provided via two right-turn only driveways on South Fourth Street.

known_conditions.narrative: The project is located within the Downtown Growth Area Boundary, and the City of San Jose is requiring a 1% annual growth factor be applied to historical count data due to the COVID-19 pandemic. The existing peak hour intersection volumes are estimated using this growth factor.

study_intersections.name: Fourth Street and San Salvador Street

recommendations.description: Provide appropriate visible warning signs and audible warning signals at the project driveways to alert pedestrians and bicyclists to vehicles exiting the site
Provide a larger radius at the top and bottom of the garage ramps, if feasible, to better serve vehicles turning simultaneously
Install convex mirrors at the top and bottom of the ramps and all blind corners of the parking garage to assist drivers with making these turns within the garage
Provide at least 15 feet of vertical clearance at the southern garage entrance in order to accommodate trucks on site
Schedule residential move-ins and move-outs during off-peak hours to minimize conflicts at the southern garage entrance to the extent possible
Provide at least 6 feet of clearance (fire access) around the entire perimeter of the building or consider the fire variance process for mitigation of non-compliance
Coordinate with City staff to determine if the proposed 20-foot-wide garage entrances, 20-foot-wide garage ramps, and 22-foot-wide drive aisles would be adequate to serve the project

Provide adequate stacking space for at least two inbound vehicles (40 to 50 feet) between the sidewalk and the garage entry gates or keep the entry gates open during the time period of the day when most inbound vehicle trips are likely to occur
Coordinate with the City of San Jose Planning Department to determine the best approach to meeting the project’s vehicle parking requirement

description.narrative: The Scripps Mercy Hospital Campus Project proposes the demolition of several existing buildings and construction of new buildings on the Scripps Mercy Hospital Campus site. The Project requires a Conditional Use Permit, Site Development Permit, and a Planned Development Permit. The Project proposes the construction of new facilities, including Hospital I, Hospital II, Hospital Support Building, Medical Office Building, and Central Energy Plant Expansion.

known_conditions.narrative: The Project is expected to generate approximately 6,086 driveway average daily trips with 350 total driveway trips during the AM peak hour and 589 total driveway trips during the PM peak hour. The Project is expected to generate 1,490 net new ADT with 74 total net new trips during the AM peak hour and 129 total net new trips during the PM peak hour.

recommendations.description: Construct an 11 ft pedestrian path to connect the Hospital I building with the Hospital II building

description.narrative: The project involves the construction of a 4-story, 155-room hotel on a 2.5-acre site in Pleasant Hill, California. The site is currently occupied by a restaurant and a vacant retail building. The project includes approximately 133 surface parking spaces and a shuttle service to the nearby BART station.

known_conditions.narrative: The project's traffic impacts were analyzed during the weekday morning and evening peak periods. No significant intersection impacts were identified, but potential impacts during the construction phase were noted.

a set of comprehensive traffic control measures, including scheduling of major truck trips and deliveries to avoid peak hours; lane closure proceedings; signs, cones, and other warning devices for drivers; and designation of construction access routes

permitted construction hours
location of construction staging

description.narrative: Vega SES 4 LLC is proposing to develop a 100-megawatt solar photovoltaic energy generation project with an integrated 100 MW battery storage project on approximately 531.53 acres of land in Imperial County, California. The project is expected to begin construction in 2022 and open in 2023. The construction of the site is estimated to take 12-18 months.

known_conditions.narrative: The study area includes several intersections and roadway segments that are expected to be affected by the project. The traffic analysis included intersections and roadway segments within Imperial County and Caltrans District 11. The County of Imperial's goal is to maintain a level of service of C or better on all County-maintained roads.

location_context.surrounding_land_use: residential and commercial to the north, residential to the west, a commercial development consisting of restaurants, an office, and retail service to the east, and retail and offices to the south

description.narrative: The proposed Pacific Coast Commons project is a mixed-use development located on Pacific Coast Highway between East Palm Avenue and East Holly Avenue. The project consists of 263 residential units and 11,252 square feet of commercial space. The project will also construct sidewalk improvements and landscaping enhancements along the project site.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing conditions analysis includes a description of the transportation system serving the project site, existing traffic volumes, and an assessment of the operating conditions at the study analysis locations. The City of El Segundo has not yet adopted new transportation impact study guidelines including the VMT metric and significance criteria in compliance with SB 743 guidelines.

issues.location: Pacific Coast Hwy & Pacific Coast Commons South Dwy

description.narrative: The project proposes to develop a hard rock quarry located east of Alta Road at the end of Calzada De La Fuente in the County of San Diego. The project is a proposal to establish a mineral resource recovery operation and associated activities to create much needed construction aggregates and materials to serve the economy of San Diego County for an approximate 90±year period. The project is located within a 438-acre ownership with extractive operations proposed on 110 acres of the site.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions are based on the 2017 traffic counts, and the study area was revised to reflect the SR-905 extension. The traffic forecasts for the year 2050 were based on the February 20, 2014 Traffic Forecasts prepared by SANDAG for the General Plan Update.

recommendations.location: Otay Mesa Road west of Alta Road to east of Enrico Drive
recommendations.description: restripe the existing two (2) lane roadway to provide one(1) lane in each direction plus a continuous center turn lane
signalize the intersection
construct a cul-de-sac at the eastern terminus to provide access to the Otay Hills project site

metadata.report_title: Otay Hills Aggregate Mining and Inert Debris Engineered Landfill Operation

description.narrative: The Arlington Mixed Use project is a development in the City of Riverside. The project's traffic study was prepared to assess potential deficiencies to traffic and circulation. The study analyzed existing and future traffic conditions.

known_conditions.narrative: The study area intersections are currently operating at an acceptable level of service during peak hours under existing conditions. The background traffic growth rate is 12.62% from 2022 to 2028, or 2.0% per year compounded annually.

study_intersections.name: Streeter Av. & El Molino Av./ Driveway 2

recommendations.description: Modify the traffic signal cycle length to provide a 130-second cycle

description.narrative: The project involves the proposed Building 1 High-Cube Transload and Short-Term Storage use and the proposed Building 2 warehouse use. The existing land use generates trips, which were subtracted from the proposed trips to develop the total net project trip generation. A traffic impact analysis will be prepared and submitted to the City.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing land use generates 14 PCE trips during the a.m. peak hour, 10 PCE trips during the p.m. peak hour, and 131 daily PCE trips. The proposed project is forecast to generate 91 a.m. peak hour PCE trips, 110 p.m. peak hour PCE trips.

recommendations.description: Add a westbound through lane. Add overlap phasing to the southbound right-turn lane.

description.narrative: The Alexan Mixed-Use Development project is located in the City of Arcadia. The project includes residential and commercial components. The transportation analysis for the project evaluates its consistency with the City of Arcadia General Plan Circulation and Infrastructure Element.

known_conditions.narrative: The City has vehicle LOS standards in its General Plan Circulation and Infrastructure Element. The study area intersections included in this analysis consist of intersections where the project would generate 51 or more peak hour trips.

recommendations.description: Provide wayfinding signage at all parking garage ingress points for customers prior to entering the garage
Provide wayfinding signage within the parking garage such that customers are directed to the ATM drive-thru, and other users of the site are channeled to parking spaces and garage exits
Restrict northbound left-turning movements onto Santa Clara Street

description.narrative: The proposed project is a Continuing Care Retirement Community (CCRC) located at 6160 Highway 12, consisting of 74 detached cottages, 528 apartments, and a 62-unit assisted living facility. The project includes onsite amenities and transportation services for residents. The site is immediately northwest of the existing Oakmont retirement community.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions were evaluated based on data collected in April 2017, prior to the Tubbs and Nuns fires. The study area includes 10 intersections, all of which are operating acceptably under existing conditions.

description.narrative: The Majestic Freeway Business Center (Building 13) is a proposed industrial warehouse development. The project is expected to generate traffic and contribute to the existing traffic conditions in the area. The development will include new connections to be constructed by the Project.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions in the area are expected to grow due to background traffic growth and cumulative development traffic. The ambient growth factor is 6.12% for 2025 traffic conditions, which is based on a 2% per year growth rate compounded over 3 years.

Add a third eastbound through lane and a third westbound through lane

known_conditions.narrative: A queuing analysis was performed for the study area off-ramps at the SR-14 Freeway at the Avenue M and Avenue N interchanges. known_conditions.narrative: The study area has multiple intersections with existing traffic volumes. The background growth rate is 2.0% per year. The project's traffic volumes will be added to the existing traffic volumes to determine the cumulative traffic conditions.

The project will add to the existing traffic conditions in the area.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions in the area are experiencing some congestion, with several intersections operating at an unacceptable level of service. The background growth rate in the area is expected to be 6.12% for Phase 1 and 14.87% for Phase 2.

description.narrative: The project proposes to redevelop a site located at 93-129 Braintree Street and 95 Everett Street in Allston by demolishing the existing on-site buildings and constructing a 12-story commercial building and a 6-story residential building. The commercial building will have ground floor retail/restaurant space, while the residential building will be a mixed-income, for-rent housing development with approximately 88 units. The project will also create new open space and streetscape improvements accessible to the public.
description.size_units: a 12-story, approximately 385,700 square-foot lab/research and development/office building and an approximately 88-unit residential building

known_conditions.narrative: The area surrounding the project site is rapidly transforming with the development of several new mixed-use developments, and the project site is located directly adjacent to the MBTA Boston Landing Commuter Rail Station. The Everett Street bridge is a critical element of the existing urban design framework for the area, providing a connection between the project site and Lower Allston.

The new clinical building will provide state-of-the-art clinical spaces for patients with complex behavioral health and medical conditions. The project aims to modernize the Franciscan Hospital for Children's campus and expand its services to meet the growing need of children with behavioral health and complex medical conditions.
description.size_units: 250,000 sq ft clinical building, 8,500 sq ft gym and connector

known_conditions.narrative: The existing campus has eight connected buildings, with the oldest buildings dating back to 1949. The campus has a large parking lot and several smaller parking zones. The surrounding area is a mix of residential and institutional land uses.

recommendations.description: Incorporate proactive TDM measures to encourage the use of non-vehicular modes of transportation
Designate a Transportation Coordinator (TC) to oversee parking and work with employees to promote the use of non-vehicular transportation modes
Join and participate in a Transportation Management Association (TMA) – likely either Longwood Collective’s CommuteWorks or the Allston-Brighton TMA
Require employers to participate in the MBTA’s Perq program and offer pre-tax transit benefits
Provide tailored transportation marketing services to encourage employee use of nearby transportation options
Hold at least two annual events promoting active transportation modes
Provide guaranteed ride home services
Make a monetary contribution to Boston’s bikeshare system and provide a standard 15-dock station on site
Provide 46 outdoor bicycle racks (92 spaces) and at least 92 secure bicycle storage spaces
Provide on-site real time transportation information on screens or monitors
Implement a parking supply reduction of on-campus parking, by reducing on-site parking from 357 spaces to about 153 spaces
Provide clearly designated spaces exclusive for carpool/vanpool parking
Commit to the implementation of an annual Transportation Monitoring Program (TMP) beginning six months after full occupancy and running for five years
Implement a TDM program to reduce traffic trips and therefore related air quality impacts
Develop a construction Indoor Air Quality Management plan during construction and prior to occupancy
Enter into a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with the MHC outlining mitigation terms for the loss of Building 7
Manage excess soils generated as a result of the planned construction in accordance with applicable regulations, including the Massachusetts Contingency Plan (MCP)

Develop a construction waste management plan to ensure that a minimal amount of waste debris is disposed of in landfills and to pursue the goal of diverting at least 75 percent of project-generated construction waste from landfills
Employ construction methodologies that ensure public safety and protect the immediately surrounding area
Replace the green ball signal indication for the center through lane on the Dean Street westbound approach with vertical green arrow to reinforce that left turns are not permitted
Install pavement markings to better define the Route 1 northbound intersection departure lane drop
Refresh worn pavement markings throughout the intersection, including crosswalks, for improved visibility and lane compliance
Conduct a full sign inventory and sign reflectivity/visibility assessment to see if additional signs need to be installed and replace any non-compliant signs
Evaluate the location of guide signs that direct motorists to Dean Street and placement of additional signage (including overhead) further north and south on Route 1
Evaluate the yellow and all red clearance times to reduce instances of red-light running and increase efficiency
Install retroreflective backplates to the signal heads to improve signal visibility
Evaluate pedestrian crossing times per MUTCD guidelines and update as needed
Re-install missing bicycle symbols at STOP bar markings on all approaches
Install advanced warning signage to improve signal awareness, particularly for Providence Highway (US Route 1) southbound drivers
Evaluate pedestrian crossing times per MUTCD guidelines and update as needed
Install "No Turn on Red" signs on the eastbound and westbound approaches due to the restricted sight distance
Install lane extensions though the intersection to guide vehicles turning left
Install lane use signage and markings to reduce the probability of overlapping paths from turning vehicles
Extend the right lane bays on both Providence Highway (US Route 1) approaches to prevent drivers from using the shoulders to reach the right-turn lane
Evaluate the yellow and all red clearance times to reduce instances of red-light running and increase efficiency
Secure corporate membership in the Neponset Valley Transportation Management Association (TMA)
Encourage the implementation of flexible and staggered work hours by tenants
Implement direct deposit and other automatic employee payroll transactions
Provide MBTA services and schedules for nearby commuter rail stations and bus stops to each tenant to distribute or post for employees
Provide bicycle racks on-site in close proximity to the building entrances for use by employees and patrons
Include additional sidewalks and/or designated pathways to the proposed buildings that connect to the system of Site walkways
Designate an on-site employee transportation coordinator to disseminate relevant TDM information to employees
Consult MassDOT regarding the ability for the Project to utilize the Workforce Transportation Program
Develop a schedule of on-site employee services, including an employee fitness center, showers, food options for employees, direct deposit of employee payroll checks, and mail drop-off boxes
Designate preferential parking locations for employees who use carpools and vanpools
Provide additional electric vehicle charging stations/outlets for use of employees and visitors
Encourage tenants to offer subsidization of MBTA bus passes to employees
Provide a guaranteed ride home program through membership in the Neponset Valley TMA

description.narrative: The 3333 California Street Mixed-Use Project involves redevelopment of a 10.25-acre site in San Francisco's Presidio Heights neighborhood. The proposed project includes residential, retail, office, child care, and associated parking uses. The project would be developed over a 7-to 15-year construction timeframe.

recommendations.description: Implement transit bulbouts
Construct transit bulbouts and relocate transit stop
Expand pedestrian bulbout and upgrade traffic signal
Relocate transit stop and construct transit bulbout
Remove bus stop and construct transit bulbouts
Improve safety through streetscape project

location_context.surrounding_land_use: varied, including residential, office, retail, and industrial areas

description.narrative: The Modified Project includes residential, office, retail, hotel, and community services uses, plus an arena, at Candlestick Point, and residential, retail, office/R&D, community services, a hotel, educational uses, a marina, and artists’ studios at Hunters Point Shipyard. The project aims to reduce vehicle miles traveled (VMT) through increased density and investment in transit service. The project's buildout is expected to reduce VMT at the site.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions in the area are characterized by varying levels of congestion, with some areas experiencing higher volumes of traffic than others. The San Francisco County Transportation Authority uses the San Francisco Chained Activity Model Process (SF-CHAMP) to estimate VMT by private automobiles and taxis for different land use types.

metadata.report_title: Addendum 5 to FEIR - Appendix D - Analysis of Transportation Effects

description.narrative: The project involves the development of a transportation model for the Market Street and Mission Street area in San Francisco. The model aims to simulate traffic conditions and transit operations. The project includes the coding of traffic signals, transit lanes, and streetcar tracks.

known_conditions.narrative: The existing traffic conditions on Market Street include a mix of private vehicles, taxis, shuttles, and trucks. The street has a transit lane that is used by buses and streetcars. The traffic signals are timed to optimize traffic flow.

recommendations.description: Add 22 flex zones on Market Street
Add short-term commercial loading spaces within 100 feet of Market Street
Create commercial loading spaces on alleys to accommodate truck loading
Implement time-of-day commercial loading restrictions
Incentivize nighttime loading and the use of smaller delivery trucks
Create up to 46 new passenger loading zones and 8 new blue accessible parking zones

description.narrative: The Central SoMa Plan is a transportation study area in San Francisco, analyzing potential impacts of projects on Muni service and regional transit agencies. The study area includes various screenlines and cordons, assessing transit capacity and pedestrian conditions. The plan aims to evaluate existing conditions and identify mitigation measures for potential impacts.

known_conditions.narrative: The study area has existing pedestrian conditions with narrow sidewalks, missing curb ramps, and closed crosswalks. The Muni downtown screenlines operate below the 85 percent capacity utilization standard, except for the Southwest screenline during the a.m. peak hour. Regional transit operators have a one-hour load factor standard of 100 percent, with BART exceeding this standard during peak hours.

recommendations.description: Enhanced Transit Funding, Transit Corridor Improvement Review, Transit Accessibility, Muni Storage and Maintenance

description.narrative: === PREAMBLE === IV. ENVIRONMENTAL SETTING, IMPACTS, AND MITIGATION MEASURES F. Noise and Vibration Setting Sound Descriptors Decibel Sound is characterized by various parameters that describe the rate of oscillation (frequency) of sound waves, the distance between successive troughs or crests in the wave, the speed that it travels, and the pressure level or energy content of a given sound.
